title: Dog Chewing on Back Foot: Causes, Redness, and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog chewing their back foot? Learn about common causes for paw redness, urgency levels, and why photos help your vet provide better care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Compulsive foot chewing in dogs is usually caused by allergies, parasites, or anxiety and is rarely an emergency unless accompanied by severe limping or signs of infection. Providing clear photos helps your veterinarian determine if your pet needs an urgent appointment or a scheduled visit.

What causes my dog to compulsively chew on their foot?

When a dog compulsively chews on their back foot, the resulting redness is often a condition called pododermatitis. This inflammation can be triggered by several factors:

  • Environmental allergies to grass or pollen
  • Food sensitivities
  • Parasitic irritations like fleas or mites
  • Foreign objects lodged between the toes
  • Displacement behaviors related to anxiety or boredom

Is compulsive foot chewing in dogs a veterinary emergency?

The urgency level for foot chewing is generally low, as it is rarely life-threatening. However, you should seek veterinary care sooner if you notice:

  • Excessive bleeding in the affected area
  • A foul odor suggesting infection
  • Sudden limping or an inability to bear weight on the leg
  • Signs of secondary bacterial or yeast infections caused by persistent chewing

How does a photo of my dog's paw help with triage?

Capturing a clear, high-resolution photo of the underside and top of the paw helps veterinary professionals assess the severity of the inflammation by looking for:

  • Interdigital cysts or localized swelling
  • Puncture wounds or foreign bodies
  • Visual evidence to determine if the condition requires an urgent or scheduled appointment

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Excessive licking, chewing, rubbing, and scratching, particularly directed at the feet, can indicate pruritus. Owners may not always recognize licking as a sign of pruritus. Diseases that begin with pruritus may lead to self-trauma and subsequent development of secondary skin lesions like alopecia and seborrhea, or infections such as bacterial or yeast pyoderma. When a dog is chewing at its back foot with associated redness, possible differentials include allergic skin disease (such as atopic dermatitis typically affecting the face and feet), parasitic infestations (like cheyletiellosis), or, rarely, underlying neurologic disorders that manifest as cutaneous lesions and self-mutilation.

Chapter: Dermatology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 882)

faqs.question: Can I use human anti-itch creams on my dog's paws?
faqs.answer: You should avoid using any human medications or creams without a veterinarian's approval, as many can be toxic if ingested when the dog licks their feet.

faqs.question: How can I stop my dog from chewing until the vet appointment?
faqs.answer: Using an Elizabethan collar (the 'cone of shame') is the most effective way to prevent your dog from further damaging the skin while you wait for a professional evaluation.

faqs.question: Could my dog's paw redness be caused by the weather?
faqs.answer: Yes, extreme heat can burn paw pads, and icy conditions or salt used on roads can cause significant chemical or thermal irritation leading to redness and chewing.
